title: Why the brain creates false narratives to explain body sensations
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:

 I was sitting in my therapist's office a few years ago, and my stomach started to knot up. My chest...
htmlContent:
The body speaks. The mind translates. Often badly.

I was sitting in my therapist's office a few years ago, and my stomach started to knot up. My chest got tight. My hands went cold. And my brain, ever the helpful narrator, immediately started spinning a story. "You're anxious about this session," it said. "You don't trust her. Something's wrong. Maybe you should leave."

I almost did. I was ready to bolt. But then my therapist, Janina Fisher trained, said something that stopped me cold. "Notice the sensation before the story. Just the physical feeling. Don't attach the meaning yet."

And I tried. I really did. But here's the thing: my brain had already built an entire screenplay. Complete with characters, conflict, and a dramatic climax. All from a little knot in my stomach.

This is what we do. All of us. Every single day. Our bodies send signals, and our brains immediately construct elaborate narratives to explain them. The problem? Those narratives are almost always wrong.

The interpreter in your skull

Let's talk about what's actually happening inside your head. You've got this incredible organ, the brain, that's constantly trying to make sense of the world. And by "make sense," I mean it's trying to keep you alive. That's its job. Survival first, accuracy second.

Peter Levine, the guy who literally wrote the book on trauma and the body, calls this the "felt sense." It's the raw, pre-verbal experience of what's happening in your body. Before language. Before story. Just sensation. But most of us never get to that level because our brains are too busy narrating.

Think about it. You feel a flutter in your chest. Your brain says, "I'm excited." Or "I'm scared." Or "I'm having a heart attack." Same flutter, different stories. And the story you choose determines everything about how you respond.

Stay with me here. I know this can feel like a lot. The science, the terminology, the implications. But underneath all of it is something simple: your body has been trying to keep you alive. Every symptom you have - the anxiety, the dissociation, the hypervigilance, the numbness - those are survival strategies. They worked. They got you here.

Pat Ogden's sensorimotor psychotherapy approach is built on this understanding. The body isn't the enemy. It's not something to override or push through. It's the primary pathway back to yourself. When we listen to what the body is doing - really listen, without judgment - we start to understand the logic of our own survival.

Right now, as you read this, can you notice your feet on the ground? Can you feel the weight of your body in the chair? That's not a small thing. That's your nervous system practicing presence. Practicing being here, in this moment, instead of back there in the memory. Every time you do that - every single time - you're building new neural pathways. You're proving to your body that now is different from then.

This isn't just philosophy. It's neuroscience. The brain's interpreter, a region called the left hemisphere's interpretive center, is constantly generating explanations for what the body is doing. And it doesn't care much about accuracy. It cares about coherence. It needs the story to make sense, even if the story is completely made up.

Here's where it gets wild. Your brain doesn't just interpret body sensations after the fact. It actually predicts them. Dan Siegel, the interpersonal neurobiology guy, talks about how the brain is a prediction machine. It takes past experiences and uses them to guess what's coming next. So if you've been traumatized, your brain is predicting danger constantly. And then it creates body sensations to match those predictions. And then it tells you a story about why you're feeling that way.

Stay with me here. This is the loop that keeps people stuck for years. Decades. The body reacts. The brain tells a story. The story reinforces the body's reaction. And you end up trapped in a narrative that has nothing to do with what's actually happening right now.

Trauma stories and the body's secret language

When you've lived through trauma, this process gets turbocharged. Your nervous system is already on high alert. Stephen Porges, the Polyvagal Theory guy, would say your vagus nerve is scanning for safety or danger constantly. And when it detects something that even vaguely resembles a past threat, your body goes into protection mode.

But here's the kicker: your brain doesn't know the difference between a real threat and a remembered one. Or even an imagined one. The body responds the same way. Heart rate goes up. Muscles tense. Breathing gets shallow. And then your brain has to explain why you're suddenly feeling like you're being chased by a predator when you're just sitting on your couch watching Netflix.

So it makes something up. "I must be stressed about work tomorrow." Or "I'm having a panic attack for no reason." Or "There's something wrong with me." The narrative becomes self-blame. Self-pathologizing. A story that keeps you small and scared.

I remember a client who would get nauseous every time she walked into her kitchen. Her brain told her she had a stomach problem. She went to doctors. Got tests. Nothing. Then we started tracking the sensation. The nausea came, she noticed, right around 6 PM. Which was when her father used to come home drunk. And the kitchen was where he'd yell at her mother. The body remembered. The brain had just forgotten the real source and made up a medical story instead.

This is why the wellness industry's obsession with "positive thinking" is so damaging. It tells you to change the story without addressing the body. You can tell yourself "I am safe" a thousand times, but if your nervous system is still in a threat response, your body won't believe you. The body doesn't speak English. It speaks sensation.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger. Levine walks through exactly how the body holds trauma and how to work with the felt sense directly. It's not about changing the story. It's about changing the body's relationship to sensation.

The stories we tell ourselves about being broken

Here's the part that really gets me. The most common false narrative trauma survivors create is "I'm broken." Or "Something is wrong with me." This story comes directly from the body. You feel overwhelmed, dysregulated, exhausted. Your brain looks for a reason. And the easiest explanation is that you're the problem.

But think about it. If your nervous system has been through hell, if it's been in survival mode for years, of course it's going to feel chaotic. Of course you're going to have strange body sensations. Of course your brain is going to create wild stories to explain them. You're not broken. You're responding exactly the way a human nervous system responds to threat and trauma. The problem isn't you. The problem is that no one taught you how to read your body's language.

Think about it this way. Your brain developed in an environment where certain things were true. Maybe love came with conditions. Maybe safety was unpredictable. Maybe the people who were supposed to protect you were the same people your nervous system needed protection from. That creates a particular kind of wiring.

Sebern Fisher's work on neurofeedback shows how these patterns literally show up in brainwave activity. Hypervigilant brains produce excess high-beta waves. Dissociative brains show too much slow-wave activity in the wrong places. This isn't metaphor. This is measurable, observable, and - critically - changeable.

I remember the first time I saw my own brain map. Seeing the dysregulation in color-coded images on a screen. Something shifted in that moment. It wasn't shame. It was relief. Because if it's in the wiring, then it's not a character flaw. It's not weakness. It's not something I chose. And if it got wired this way through experience, then experience can rewire it. Slowly. Imperfectly. But really.

Gabor Mate talks about this constantly. He says trauma isn't what happens to you. It's what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you. The body's response. The nervous system's adaptation. And then the story you tell yourself about all of it.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score. Van der Kolk lays out the science in a way that makes you feel less crazy. Less alone. He shows how the body holds memory and how we can work with it directly. It's dense but worth it.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate. Magnesium supports the nervous system and can help with the physical sensations of anxiety. It's not a cure. Nothing is. But it can take the edge off so you have more capacity to work with the body directly.

I want you to notice something. Right now. What's happening in your body as you read this? Maybe your shoulders are tight. Maybe your jaw is clenched. Maybe your stomach is doing that thing. Your brain is probably already spinning a story about it. "I'm tense because this article is making me uncomfortable." Or "I'm fine, nothing's happening." Just notice the sensation before the story. That's where the real work lives.

Learning to sit with sensation without the story

So how do we break the loop? How do we stop the brain from creating false narratives? The answer is counterintuitive. You don't stop the narratives. You just stop believing them so quickly.

Here's a practice. When you notice a body sensation, any sensation, just name it without the story. "Tightness in chest." "Fluttering in stomach." "Heat in face." That's it. No "because." No "I must be..." Just the raw data. It feels ridiculous at first. Your brain will fight you. It wants to explain. It wants to narrate. But you can gently say, "Not yet. Just the sensation."

Over time, something shifts. You start to realize that sensations are just sensations. They come and go. They don't need a story. They don't need to mean anything. They're just the body doing its thing. And when you stop attaching meaning, the sensations often dissipate faster. Because they're not being fed by the narrative anymore.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? by Oprah Winfrey and Bruce Perry. It shifts the question from "What's wrong with you?" to "What happened to you?" That one shift can change how you interpret every body sensation. Suddenly the fluttering isn't a sign of brokenness. It's a sign of adaptation. Of survival.

I won't pretend this is easy. It's not. Your brain has been telling you stories for decades. Those narratives are deeply ingrained. They feel like truth. But they're not. They're just your brain trying to make sense of a nervous system that's been through a lot.

And here's the uncomfortable truth I have to leave you with: you may never fully stop the false narratives. Your brain will keep generating them. That's what brains do. But you can change your relationship to them. You can stop being the actor in the story and start being the observer of the body that's having the sensation. That shift, small as it sounds, is everything.

Your body isn't lying to you. Your brain is just a terrible translator. But you can learn to read the original language.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate - A supplement that supports nervous system regulation and can help calm physical sensations of anxiety.
  • Waking the Tiger - Peter Levine's foundational book on somatic approaches to healing trauma through the body.
  • The Body Keeps the Score - Bessel van der Kolk's look at how trauma lives in the body and what to do about it.
  • What Happened to You? - A compassionate reframing of trauma that shifts from self-blame to understanding.
